result:
I find myself thinking about lichen. Not just idly, but with a persistent, low-grade fascination. It’s not a subject I’m an expert in—far from it. I just keep noticing it, and the noticing brings a peculiar kind of quiet.

Lichen is the ultimate collaborator. It’s not one thing, but a partnership: a fungus and an alga (or a cyanobacterium) living so closely intertwined that they become a new, unique organism. The fungus provides structure, a home. The alga, through photosynthesis, provides food. They can survive where neither could alone—on sun-scorched rock, on wind-whipped tree bark, in Arctic tundra. They are pioneers, the first to settle on bare stone, beginning the slow, patient work of creating soil from nothing but mineral and time.

There’s a humility to lichen. It doesn’t rush. Its growth is measured in millimeters per year. It doesn’t fight for space with loud, showy flowers; it simply persists. In a world obsessed with speed, growth-hacking, and disruptive innovation, lichen offers a different model: resilience through cooperation, success through patience, strength in symbiosis.

I see it on the old stone wall by my favorite walking path. A map of muted greens, silvery blues, and ochre spreads across the granite. From a distance, it looks like a stain, a shadow. Up close, it’s a whole landscape. Some are crusty, like paint flaking off the rock itself (crustose). Some are leafy, like miniature lettuce clinging to the surface (foliose). Some are shrubby, tiny branching towers no taller than a fingernail (fruticose). It’s a silent, sprawling city, a testament to a different timescale.

I think we’ve lost the art of that timescale. My own life is measured in notifications, quarterly goals, and the relentless churn of a 24-hour news cycle. Lichen doesn’t know or care about any of that. A lichen colony on that wall might be decades old, possibly centuries. It has weathered storms, droughts, and the tread of countless passersby, simply by being flexible, by being a partnership.

And that’s the other thing: the partnership isn’t always peaceful. It’s a controlled parasitism as much as a mutualism. The fungus *harnesses* the alga, enclosing it, directing it. The alga’s world becomes the fungal walls. It’s a marriage, with all the complexity that implies—dependency, mutual benefit, and a certain inescapability. They are stuck with each other, and that very stuckness is the source of their extraordinary power.

Maybe my fixation is a form of longing. A longing for that kind of deep, foundational integration. To be part of something so fundamental that your individual identity blurs into something more durable and more beautiful. To be resilient not because you have a thick skin, but because you are, at your core, a collaboration. To measure your life not in achievements, but in the slow, sure accumulation of quiet presence.

Lichen is also an indicator. It can’t survive in toxic air. Its presence speaks of clean, healthy environments. So, on that old wall, it’s not just a life form; it’s a message. A green, breathing "all clear."

I don’t know what to *do* with this knowledge about lichen. I’m not going to become a bryologist. But I’ve started to seek it out. A glance at a gravestone, a tree limb, a forgotten roof tile. In each patch, I see a silent, stubborn argument against loneliness and hurry. A living proof that the most successful strategy on this ancient planet might not be competition, but a kind of radical, patient, and utterly inseparable togetherness.

It’s a good thought to hold onto. A small, quiet, and very old truth, growing in the corners.
